elements.sent-2411-001: There have been indications of several new resonances at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC).
elements.sent-2411-002: Two of them, at about 95 GeV and 650 GeV, have been indicated by more than one experiments and have reached statistical significance worthy of a serious investigation.
elements.sent-2411-003: Conservatively using only the numbers given by the experimental collaborations, we find combined global significances around 3$\sigma$ and 4$\sigma$ respectively for the 95~GeV and 650~GeV putative resonances.
elements.sent-2411-004: There are some more which may soon become very significant.
elements.sent-2411-005: We show that the data on only the 650 GeV resonance, assuming they stand the test of time, predict the existence of a doubly-charged scalar, and make the extensions of the scalar sector like those by gauge singlet scalars, the 2-Higgs doublet models or the Georgi-Machacek model, highly disfavored.
elements.sent-2411-006: We provide the readers with a minimalistic model that can explain all the indications.
elements.sent-2411-007: Such a model can also accommodate the hints of a singly charged scalar at about 375 GeV, and a doubly charged scalar at about 450 GeV, as found by both the major LHC Collaborations, the combined global significance for each of them being above $2.5\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our analysis comes with the obvious caveat that the allowed parameter space that we find depends on the available data on all the new resonances, and may change in future.
elements.sent-2411-009: Twelve years after the discovery of the Higgs boson \cite{ATLAS:2012yve, CMS:2012qbp , some more interesting hints about new resonances have been found by both the ATLAS and the CMS Collaborations \cite{ATLAS:2017tlw,CMS:2017jkd, CMS:2018mmw, CMS:2015ocq, CMS:2018cyk, CMS:2022rbd, CMS:2022goy,ATLAS:2020tlo, ATLAS:2021kog, CMS:2022bcb, CMS:2022tgk, CMS:2019pzc, ATLAS:2022wti, ATLAS:2023zkt at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC).}}

elements.sent-2411-018: We will assume all resonances to be of spin-0 nature.
elements.sent-2411-019: They have been found to have nonzero coupling with the Standard Model (SM) gauge bosons, so the mass eigenstates can hardly be dominated by gauge singlets.
elements.sent-2411-020: We will show that they cannot come from simple multi-Higgs doublet extensions either.
elements.sent-2411-021: In fact, the apparent indications tend to rule out, or at least disfavor, a number of minimal extensions of the scalar sector of the SM.
elements.sent-2411-022: There are three major constraints on any such scalar extension.
elements.sent-2411-023: The first one is the existence of custodial symmetry (CS), parametrised by the $\rho$-parameter.
elements.sent-2411-024: In the SM, $\rho=1$ at the tree-level, but the Yukawa interaction in the SM breaks the CS.
elements.sent-2411-025: Once the radiative corrections are incorporated, the $\rho$-parameter shifts from unity, the dominant contribution coming from the top quark loop and being proportional to $m_t^2$.
elements.sent-2411-026: To absorb this correction, one defines the parameter $\rho_0$, so that in the SM, $\rho_0$ is unity by definition.
elements.sent-2411-027: The experimental value is close to unity~\cite{ParticleDataGroup:2024cfk : \begin{eqnarray}\rho_0 = 1.00031 \pm 0.00019\,, \label{eq:rho-expt}\end{eqnarray} so CS must be close to an exact symmetry of nature.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Models with SU(2) singlet and doublet scalar multiplets respect CS; it is also possible to include larger multiplets of scalars in a way that CS is respected.
elements.sent-2411-029: The most famous example of this genre is the Georgi-Machacek (GM) model \cite{Georgi:1985nv , where a complex triplet and a real triplet of scalars engender $\rho=1$ at the tree-level\footnote{The CS is respected in the GM model even at the one-loop level if one considers only the SU(2) part of the weak interaction.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Once $\rm U(1)_Y$ is introduced and gauged, the tadpole diagrams break the CS.. .
elements.sent-2411-031: Scalar extensions that violate CS must have very small vacuum expectation values (VEV) of the CS-breaking fields; thus, they are unlikely to be singly produced by gauge boson fusion, or decay to the gauge bosons with any significant branching ratio (BR), as the (neutral) scalar-gauge-gauge coupling is always proportional to the VEV of that scalar.
elements.sent-2411-032: The GM model is interesting in the sense that this is the minimal model that preserves the CS and introduces both singly and doubly charged scalars in the spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-033: One may even construct a more generalised version of the GM model \cite{KMP22 where the CS is respected in the gauge sector only but not in the scalar sector, {\em i.e. , the components of the custodial scalar multiplets can potentially be non-degenerate.}}

elements.sent-2411-202: Adding just an $\rm SU(2)_L$ doublet to the SM doublet would not work either.

elements.sent-2411-224: We would like to propose a new model here, which we call Two-Higgs Doublet extended Georgi-Machacek Model (2HDeGM for short).
elements.sent-2411-225: The extension from the canonical GM model occurs in two steps.
elements.sent-2411-226: First, we add one more $\rm SU(2)_L$ scalar doublet with the same weak hypercharge as the first doublet.
elements.sent-2411-227: Second, we impose CS only on the gauge part of the Lagrangian and not on the scalar potential, in the same vein as Ref.\ \cite{KMP22 .}
elements.sent-2411-228: The latter condition removes the mass degeneracy among the scalar states in any nonsinglet custodial multiplet.
elements.sent-2411-229: Thus, 2HDeGM consists of two SU(2) doublets, $\Phi_1$ and $\Phi_2$ (with VEVs $v_1$ and $v_2$ respectively), a real triplet $\Xi$ and a complex triplet $X$ (both with VEV $u$).
elements.sent-2411-230: The CP-even neutral members of these multiplets are denoted by $\phi_1^0$, $\phi_2^0$, $\xi^0$, and $\chi^0$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-231: This is a minimal extension of the canonical GM model, and by construction preserves CS.

elements.sent-2411-234: There are at least four two-Higgs doublet models (2HDM) that forbid tree-level flavor-changing neutral current through some discrete symmetries on the scalar sector.

elements.sent-2411-438: Over the last few years, there have been strong hints of more resonances from the LHC experiments, at around 95~GeV and 650~GeV, and possibly at 320~GeV and 400~GeV, even though none of them are officially confirmed as yet.

elements.sent-2411-443: Another resonance at around 400 GeV, which is in all probability a CP-odd neutral scalar, has also breached the $3\sigma$ mark, and the significance of the one at 320 GeV is just below $3\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-444: We first show that the coupling strengths of these new scalars to the weak gauge bosons predict the existence of doubly charged scalars, if the underlying theory is renormalizable and the unitarity bounds respected.
elements.sent-2411-445: This, in turn, leads us to discard the simplest scalar extensions like those with $\rm SU(2)_L$ singlets and doublets, and help us to arrive at the minimal model, with two $\rm SU(2)_L$ doublets, one real triplet, and one complex triplet.
elements.sent-2411-446: The triplet structure is similar to the Georgi-Machacek model, with identical VEVs for the triplet fields to ensure $\rho=1$ at the tree level, except that the custodial symmetry should not be respected in the scalar sector and hence the custodial multiplets are no longer mass degenerate.
elements.sent-2411-447: We call this the 2-Higgs Doublet extended Georgi-Machacek (2HDeGM) model.
elements.sent-2411-448: This model accommodates not only all the neutral scalars, both CP-even and CP-odd, but also singly and doubly charged scalars for which there are some mild hints from both ATLAS and CMS Collaborations.
elements.sent-2411-449: While the model may seem to contain a lot of fields and free parameters, we show that the existing data, taken at face value, severely constrains the allowed parameter space, without even going into a detailed discussion of the scalar potential and resulting constraints.
